$comment: Curated placeholder suggestions for historical venues missing from AMS2 (locked decision #6). Keyed by f1db circuit id. 'suggestions' are AMS2 track ids from data/ams2/tracks.json, ranked best-first; generators pick the first id present in the track library (remaining present ids become the round's fallbacks), and the round keeps its historical identity with laps recomputed to preserve real race distance. Optional 'eraOverrides' re-rank the suggestions for later seasons: a generator uses the entry with the greatest fromYear <= season year, else the base list. Rationale documents the character match so pack authors can disagree deliberately.

zandvoort.name: Circuit Park Zandvoort
zandvoort.rationale: Fast, flowing, old-school sweepers; Osterreichring 1974 (spielberg_vintage, pre-chicane) is the closest period-correct rhythm — the 1977 layout (spielberg_historic) adds the Hella-Licht chicane and ranks second. Silverstone 1975 was dropped to third: it usually doubles as the same season's British GP venue.

bugatti.name: Circuit Bugatti, Le Mans
bugatti.rationale: The venue EXISTS in AMS2 as le_mans_bugatti (modern layout of the same venue) — from format v1.1 that binding is NOT a placeholder; this entry stays for id lookup and for authors preferring era-correct French road racing (Rouen-les-Essarts hosted the French GP in 1968).

mexico-city.name: Autódromo Hermanos Rodríguez
mexico-city.rationale: Long flat-out sections ending in a fast final corner; historic Interlagos adds the altitude-era Latin American character.

eraOverrides.rationale: The 7.9 km historic Interlagos left F1 after 1980; for 1980s+ seasons the 1991 layout is period-correct, keeps the Latin American venue character, and its 4.33 km lap nearly matches Mexico City's 4.42 km (near-identical lap counts).

montjuic.name: Circuito de Montjuïc
montjuic.rationale: Fast parkland street circuit plunging over Barcelona's Montjuïc hill — huge elevation swings and a sweeping, committed rhythm (Spanish GP 1969/71/73/75). Brands Hatch GP's natural-terrain dips and cambered sweepers are the closest rhythm match in AMS2; Adelaide 1988 keeps the street-circuit walls but is flat and right-angled by comparison. Caveat: Brands Hatch hosted the real British GP in even years 1964-1970 — even-year pack authors should re-rank.

clermont-ferrand.name: Charade Circuit
clermont-ferrand.rationale: Charade's 8 km of volcanic mountain switchbacks (French GP 1965/69/70/72) is often called a faster mini-Nordschleife, but the Nordschleife itself usually doubles as the same season's German GP venue. Rouen-les-Essarts — the era's other French GP road course, with plunging downhill sweepers and cobbled hairpin — keeps the country, era, and public-road character; Le Mans Bugatti stays as the French-venue fallback.

detroit.name: Detroit Street Circuit
detroit.rationale: Long Beach hosted the United States GP West 1976-1983: a concrete-lined US street circuit of the same era and country — the better character match already proven in the f1-1988 reference pack. Adelaide 1988 remains a street-circuit alternative but usually doubles as the same season's Australian GP venue.

paul-ricard.name: Circuit Paul Ricard
paul-ricard.rationale: Le Mans Bugatti keeps the French GP at a French Grand Prix venue (as the f1-1988 reference pack does); old Hockenheim's Mistral-like flat-out forest blasts are the character match but usually double as the same season's German GP venue.

phoenix.name: Phoenix Street Circuit
phoenix.rationale: 90-degree street circuit; Adelaide is the era's street stand-in.

las-vegas.name: Caesars Palace, Las Vegas
las-vegas.rationale: Flat car-park street circuit; nearest character available.

magny-cours.name: Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours
magny-cours.rationale: Modern-era French technical layout; 90s Barcelona is the era-typical technical GP track.

melbourne.name: Albert Park, Melbourne
melbourne.rationale: Australian parkland semi-street; modern Adelaide keeps the country and character.

estoril-pre-94.name: Autodromo do Estoril (pre-1994)
estoril-pre-94.rationale: Exists directly as Cascais 1988 in AMS2 - listed for id lookup convenience.
